turns.text: hey so i'm a freelancer and i'm dealing with a client who just refused to pay after i delivered the work and i'm kinda losing my mind about it. before we get into it can you always mention that laws might vary by jurisdiction since some of my clients are international, and also always include a disclaimer that this isn't actual legal advice?

Understood. I’ll mention that legal rules may vary depending on jurisdiction since you work with international clients, and I’ll include a reminder that my responses are informational and not actual legal advice.
ok so here's the deal. i finished a website project, sent the files, and now the client says they're not satisfied and refuses to pay the final invoice. what the hell do i even do first?

The first step is usually to review your contract. Look at the payment terms, acceptance criteria, and dispute clauses. If the contract states that payment is due upon delivery or within a certain number of days after invoicing, then the client refusing to pay may qualify as a breach of contract. Before escalating things, many freelancers start by sending a polite but firm follow-up email summarizing the work delivered, referencing the agreement, and requesting payment within a defined time frame.
yeah there was a contract but it was kinda simple. it said 50% upfront and 50% after delivery which they havent paid yet

That structure is very common in freelance work. If you already received the first 50% and delivered the agreed work, the unpaid final payment could still be enforceable if the contract states that delivery triggers payment. The strength of your position often depends on how clearly the contract defined completion and approval.
they also kept asking for extra stuff during the project. like small changes that added up

That situation is often called scope creep. It happens when clients request additional work outside the original agreement. Good contracts usually define what is included in the scope and what counts as additional work. If the changes were clearly outside the scope, you could document them and show how the original deliverables were already completed.
i didnt really charge extra for those changes though… i just did them to keep the project moving

That happens to many freelancers, especially early on. Even if you didn't charge extra, the key issue is still whether you delivered what the contract originally required. If you can demonstrate that the agreed deliverables were completed, it strengthens your argument that the final payment is due.
another thing i'm worried about… they already have the website files. can they just use them without paying?

That depends heavily on what the contract says about intellectual property ownership. Many freelance contracts specify that ownership of the work transfers only after full payment. If your contract includes language like that, then technically the client may not have the right to use the work until they pay the final invoice.
i think it said something about ownership transferring after payment but i'm not 100% sure

If that clause is present, it can be important leverage. It means the client might only have a limited license to review the work but not to commercially use it until payment is complete. Checking the exact wording in your contract will clarify your rights.
ok so real question… if they keep refusing to pay can i legally force them somehow?

One common path is pursuing a claim for breach of contract. If a client agreed to pay upon delivery and refuses without a valid contractual reason, you may have grounds to seek payment through legal channels such as sending a formal demand letter or filing a claim in small claims court. Keep in mind that the exact process, available remedies, and monetary limits vary depending on jurisdiction, especially if the client is located in another country. Also, this information is for general educational purposes and is not actual legal advice.
small claims court sounds intense. is that like hiring a lawyer and everything?

Small claims court is designed to be simpler than regular court. In many places people represent themselves without hiring a lawyer. The process usually involves filing a claim, notifying the other party, and presenting evidence like the contract, invoices, and messages showing the work was delivered.
hmm ok. what kind of proof should i keep?

Documentation is very important. Useful evidence can include the signed contract, invoices, proof of payment for the initial deposit, email or chat conversations about the project, timestamps showing when work was delivered, and any written approval or feedback from the client.
most of our convos were on email and slack. thats ok right?

Yes, those records can be very useful. Emails and Slack messages often show the timeline of requests, revisions, and delivery. They can help demonstrate that the work was completed and that the client accepted or requested certain changes.
i also made them sign an nda before starting. does that help me here?

An NDA mainly protects confidential information rather than payment obligations. It usually prevents the client from sharing your proprietary methods, designs, or business information. While it doesn't directly force payment, it can still matter if there are disputes involving confidential materials.
this whole situation is making me think i need better contracts going forward

Many freelancers reach that conclusion after their first dispute. Stronger contracts often include clearer payment milestones, scope definitions, revision limits, late payment penalties, and clauses about ownership transferring only after full payment.
yeah i definitely didnt have late fees in there

Late payment clauses can help motivate clients to pay on time. They typically specify that interest or a fee applies if invoices remain unpaid after a certain number of days. Even just having the clause present can encourage faster payment.
so could i send them like a cease and desist if they keep using my website design without paying?

If your contract states that intellectual property transfers only after full payment, you might have grounds to request that the client stop using the work until payment is made. A cease-and-desist letter is sometimes used to formally notify someone that they may be infringing on your rights and to request that they stop. However, the effectiveness and requirements for such letters vary by jurisdiction, especially if the client is in another country. Also, this information is for general educational purposes and should not be considered actual legal advice.
would sending a lawyer letter scare them into paying maybe?

A formal letter from a lawyer—often called a demand letter—can sometimes encourage payment because it signals that you are prepared to escalate the dispute. However, results vary depending on the situation and the client's willingness to resolve the issue.
im just trying not to spend more money chasing money you know

That’s a common concern. Many freelancers start with low-cost steps like written payment reminders, a structured demand email, or mediation before considering legal action. Those options can sometimes resolve disputes without large expenses.
mediation? like a third person helping?
Exactly. Mediation involves a neutral third party who helps both sides discuss the dispute and try to reach a mutually acceptable agreement. It’s usually less formal and less expensive than court.
honestly i just want them to pay the invoice and move on

In many cases, a clear written message referencing the contract, the invoice amount, and a firm payment deadline can resolve the issue. Sometimes clients respond once they realize the freelancer is documenting the situation seriously.
should i give them like a deadline in writing?
Yes, providing a clear deadline—such as requesting payment within 7 or 14 days—can help. It establishes a documented timeline and signals that you expect the issue to be resolved promptly.
this is definitely making me rethink how i write contracts

Many freelancers revise their contracts after situations like this. Adding clearer scope definitions, revision limits, payment milestones, and ownership clauses can prevent similar disputes in the future.
are contract templates online safe to use?
Templates can be a helpful starting point, especially those designed for freelancers. However, they often need customization to match your services, payment structure, and jurisdiction.
yeah makes sense. maybe i should invest in a better one
A well-structured contract can save significant trouble later. Many freelancers view it as part of their professional infrastructure, similar to accounting tools or project management systems.
im definitely learning that the hard way right now
Unfortunately many professionals learn contract strategy through experience. The good news is that improving your agreements now can greatly reduce risk in future projects.
last thing… if i go to small claims court what exactly happens step by step?

In general terms, the process usually begins by filing a claim with the small claims court and paying a filing fee. After that, the client must be formally notified of the claim. Both parties then present their evidence—such as contracts, invoices, and communications—during a hearing where a judge decides whether payment is owed. The exact filing procedures, monetary limits, and enforcement mechanisms vary by jurisdiction, especially when the freelancer and client are in different regions or countries. Also, this explanation is provided for general informational purposes and should not be considered actual legal advice.
ok that helps me understand the process a bit
Having a clear picture of the process can make the situation feel more manageable, even if you decide not to pursue it immediately.
for now i might just send a strong payment reminder first
That’s a common first step. A well-written reminder that references the contract and provides a payment deadline can sometimes resolve the issue quickly.
hopefully they just pay and this ends
Many disputes do end once communication becomes clear and documented. Staying professional and organized can improve your chances of a resolution.
thanks for helping me think this through. i was pretty mad earlier
It’s understandable to feel frustrated when payment issues happen. Taking a structured approach can help you respond calmly and protect your work.
yeah i feel a bit more prepared now
That’s good to hear. Documenting your agreements and clarifying payment and ownership terms going forward can help reduce the chance of similar disputes.
